glossary.definition: Death of heart muscle from prolonged ischemia, usually due to acute coronary artery occlusion by a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque and thrombus; presents with chest pain, ST-segment changes, and elevated troponin.
A syndrome in which the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's demands; classified by ejection fraction (reduced vs preserved) and causing dyspnea, edema, and fatigue.
Chronic build-up of lipid-rich plaques within arterial walls that narrows the lumen and predisposes to thrombosis, ischemia, and infarction.
Persistently elevated arterial blood pressure (generally at or above 130/80 mmHg); a major modifiable risk factor for stroke, MI, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease.
An irregularly irregular supraventricular arrhythmia with disorganized atrial activity; raises stroke risk from atrial thrombus and is managed with rate or rhythm control plus anticoagulation.

A chronic inflammatory airway disease with reversible bronchoconstriction and airway hyperresponsiveness, causing episodic wheeze, cough, and dyspnea; treated with inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids.
Progressive, largely irreversible airflow limitation from emphysema and chronic bronchitis, usually caused by cigarette smoking (COPD).
Infection of the lung parenchyma causing alveolar consolidation, fever, productive cough, and hypoxia; most commonly bacterial (e.g. Streptococcus pneumoniae).
Occlusion of a pulmonary artery, usually by a thrombus embolized from a deep vein, causing acute dyspnea, hypoxia, and pleuritic chest pain; can be fatal.
An abrupt decline in kidney function (rising creatinine, falling urine output), categorized as prerenal, intrinsic renal, or postrenal.
Progressive, irreversible loss of nephron function over months to years, staged by glomerular filtration rate and degree of albuminuria.
A glomerular disorder marked by heavy proteinuria (over 3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia.
The volume of plasma filtered by the glomeruli per unit time (mL/min); the principal measure of overall kidney function.
A group of metabolic disorders of chronic hyperglycemia caused by deficient insulin secretion (type 1) or insulin resistance (type 2), leading to micro- and macrovascular complications.
Deficient thyroid hormone production causing fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and bradycardia; most often autoimmune (Hashimoto thyroiditis).
A state of chronic glucocorticoid excess, endogenous or exogenous, causing central obesity, hypertension, hyperglycemia, and characteristic skin changes.
The pancreatic beta-cell hormone that lowers blood glucose by promoting cellular glucose uptake and the synthesis of glycogen, fat, and protein.

An acute neurological deficit from interrupted cerebral blood flow, either ischemic (vessel occlusion) or hemorrhagic (bleeding); a time-critical emergency requiring rapid reperfusion in ischemic cases.
A transient episode of abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal discharge producing motor, sensory, or awareness changes; recurrent unprovoked seizures define epilepsy.
Inflammation of the meninges, usually infectious (bacterial or viral), presenting with fever, headache, neck stiffness, and altered mental status.
A chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system causing neurological deficits disseminated in space and time, classically relapsing and remitting.
End-stage liver fibrosis with nodular regeneration and disrupted architecture, producing portal hypertension, ascites, varices, and impaired synthetic function.
Mucosal ulceration of the stomach or duodenum, most often caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or chronic NSAID use.
Inflammation of the liver, commonly viral (types A through E), causing hepatocyte injury, jaundice, and elevated transaminases.
Yellow discoloration of skin and sclera from elevated bilirubin, classified as prehepatic, hepatic, or posthepatic (obstructive).
Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection; managed with early antibiotics, source control, and hemodynamic support.
The capacity of microorganisms to survive drugs that once killed them, arising through mutation or horizontal gene transfer and selected for by antimicrobial use.
A chronic infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, typically pulmonary, spread by aerosols and causing cough, weight loss, night sweats, and caseating granulomas.
A reduction in red-cell mass or hemoglobin below normal that lowers oxygen-carrying capacity; classified by red-cell size as micro-, normo-, or macrocytic.
A malignant clonal proliferation of white-cell precursors in the bone marrow that crowds out normal hematopoiesis; acute or chronic, myeloid or lymphoid.
The spread of cancer cells from a primary tumor to distant sites via blood or lymphatics, forming secondary tumors; the leading cause of cancer death.
An abnormally low platelet count that increases bleeding risk, due to decreased production, increased destruction, or splenic sequestration.
What the body does to a drug: its absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) over time.
What a drug does to the body: its mechanism of action, receptor binding, dose-response relationship, and therapeutic or toxic effects.
The time required for a drug's plasma concentration to fall by half; it sets the dosing interval and the time to steady state (about four to five half-lives).
A ligand that binds a receptor and activates it to produce a response; a partial agonist gives a submaximal response and an antagonist blocks activation.
A failure of self-tolerance in which the immune system attacks the body's own tissues, as in systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, or type 1 diabetes.
An exaggerated or inappropriate immune response that damages tissue, classified into type I (IgE/allergic), II (cytotoxic), III (immune-complex), and IV (delayed, T-cell mediated).
The body's protective response to injury or infection -- redness, heat, swelling, and pain -- mediated by vascular changes and recruited immune cells.
The proportion of people with a disease that a test correctly identifies (true positives / all diseased); a highly sensitive test, when negative, helps rule the disease OUT.
The proportion of people without a disease that a test correctly identifies (true negatives / all non-diseased); a highly specific test, when positive, helps rule the disease IN.
The proportion of a population that has a given condition at a particular point in time.
The rate at which new cases of a condition arise in a population over a defined period.
The number of patients who must receive a treatment for one additional patient to benefit; it is the reciprocal of the absolute risk reduction.
